title: Scaling Your Business and Scaling Your Team
summary: Erin walked participants through a structured framework for scaling both their construction business and their team, covering five key pillars: baseline focus, product clarity, commitment, execution, and analysis. She then addressed team building by team size, the importance of building an intentional ecosystem of professionals, and core operational systems including budgeting, scheduling, communication, cash flow, and standards. The session included live Q&A where participants brought real challenges around hiring project managers, managing difficult clients and designers, organizing systems, and defining internal roles.

teachings.explanation: Scaling a business requires five sequential steps: establishing a baseline focus (pick one thing), defining your product, committing fully to the strategy, executing relentlessly, and analyzing results. This cycle should be repeated continuously, even in micro-moments throughout the day.
teachings.quote: Execution matters most. We can talk about all the other stuff and they're important, but execution matters most.

teachings.explanation: Everyone you work with — architects, designers, trades, consultants — should be an intentional choice, not accidental. Proactively identifying and pursuing relationships with top professionals in your market is how you grow and elevate your work over time.
teachings.quote: Everyone you work with is an intentional move, period. That's how you grow.

teachings.explanation: Different team sizes require different leadership moves: solo owner does everything; 1-3 people means the first hire should be a money person; 3-6 people means adding field support; 6-10 means the owner steps back and delegates; 10+ means forming micro-teams each responsible for a set of projects.
teachings.quote: Cash flow keeps you in business. First hire is money person.

teachings.explanation: You must commit fully to your strategy while remaining agile enough to adjust when things change. In construction, the market and project conditions shift constantly, so stubbornly staying the course is dangerous. The goal is decisive commitment combined with rapid adaptation.
teachings.quote: You want to lock in and be agile. Don't be fragile and be agile.

teachings.concept: Four-Week Schedule as the Communication Sweet Spot
teachings.explanation: Providing clients with a four-week rolling project schedule is the most effective cadence — long enough to give meaningful visibility and time to make decisions, but short enough to remain realistic and credible. Two weeks is too short; six months is too uncertain to be trusted.
teachings.quote: If I give them a six-month projection, we all know that's BS. It's not going to... we live in a building world. Things change every day.

teachings.explanation: A 'bridge' role sits between project managers and money managers, handling modifications, approvals, coordination, and communication that falls outside either core function. This role maintains continuity, prevents gaps, and allows the company to stay lean by cross-training.
teachings.quote: Modifications person is this bridge in between. So that's all that person does is project managers document everything. She then collects the information, puts the modification document together.

teachings.explanation: Holding to high standards — even on unseen details — builds a culture where the team knows exactly what is expected. Each time you raise your standard and it becomes the new baseline, the entire company's quality level rises over time.
teachings.quote: Increase your standards, your level of standards. That becomes the new baseline. Increase it again. That becomes the new baseline. Then your team knows it.

teachings.explanation: Staying lean — having fewer, more versatile people who can perform multiple roles — protects the business during downturns and allows you to pay team members more. Cross-training ensures continuity when someone is absent and prevents single points of failure.
teachings.quote: I will stay as lean as humanly possible for the rest of my life, which has been fun now because it's an operating system that works.

teachings.concept: Client Communication Framed Around Their Goals
teachings.explanation: When pushing clients or designers to make decisions or meet deadlines, always frame the urgency in terms of how delays affect the client's own project goals and budget — not the builder's internal operations. This approach is more persuasive and preserves the relationship.
teachings.quote: The first part of the communication is always about how it affects them... the way we frame getting answers is how it affects the client.

teachings.explanation: Erin updated her budgeting process to include three columns indicating the certainty level of each bid: hard bid ready to build, bid with details yet to be developed, and historical data estimates. This gives the whole team visibility into what is confirmed versus what still carries financial risk.
teachings.quote: Column two is we have a bid. Details need to be developed... that means the money is not certain.

actionable_advice: Pick one baseline focus for your business — one product, one niche — and build your strategy around that single focus before expanding.
Use a 90-day subcontractor arrangement to evaluate new project manager hires before bringing them on as employees, and run them through a scorecard during that period.
Build and maintain a written top-five list of architects, designers, and trades you want to work with, and actively pursue those relationships even years before landing them.
Implement a four-week rolling schedule for clients and include a 'hot list' in red at the bottom of each weekly report to highlight urgent decisions needed from the client.
Add three price-certainty columns to your budget template: hard bid confirmed, bid with details to be developed, and historical data estimates.
Convert your document storage and templates to Google Docs or Google Sheets to enable real-time collaboration, AI compatibility, and consistent access across the team.
Create a standard template project folder so every new project starts with the same file names, templates, and structure — eliminating inconsistency when onboarding new team members.
Establish non-negotiable communication rhythms: weekly reports from project managers, mid-month draw packages from money managers, and a defined approval timeline two weeks prior to each draw.
When a client or designer is missing deadlines, have a direct one-on-one conversation — in person or over coffee — to make sure they understand the real project impact of delays.
Go through the scaling scorecard in the Builder's Edge playbook honestly, with your team or alone, to identify where your business currently scores and what areas need work.
Require lien releases for any payment over $5,000 and use DocuSign or in-person sign-off at the time of payment.
client_challenges: Ryan is managing projects for the first time as an owner and feels he is not performing as well as his existing project manager, while simultaneously scaling from three to five or six jobs at higher price points.

Ryan's clients and designers are consistently missing selection deadlines, creating downstream delays for trades and putting pressure on the project manager despite extensive upfront communication systems.
Ryan is unsure whether the high-pressure, all-hands-on-deck dynamic at the $1M-$4M project level is the norm or a sign of structural problems in his business.
Leanne asked how to organize, house, and roll out systems and processes so they are accessible and consistently used, especially when onboarding new employees.

Tiffany asked how to manage the handoff of design information from architects and interior designers into the builder's own systems, given that every designer delivers information in a different format.
Denise asked which team member should be responsible for managing subcontracts and lien waivers, and how to streamline that process.
Renee asked at what team size a bridge person becomes necessary and how the PM and owner handled the communication and money side before that role existed.
notable_quotes: On execution: 'Execution matters most. We can talk about all the other stuff and they're important, but execution matters most. We've all been in the meetings with amazing ideas. And later on, we're like, well, I thought about that, but you didn't do anything with it.'
On staying agile: 'You want to lock in and be agile. Don't be fragile and be agile.'

On the $1M-$4M client dynamic: 'The one to four million homeowners — that's the world in which the investment being made is close to their net worth. So there's a real stress there. And so everything is urgent between one and four million.'

On building trade relationships: 'If you show up, we're going to be ready for you... you don't lose money every time you're on a job site and it's not ready. That is the truest form of a relationship that you can build in construction.'
On raising standards: 'How you make one decision is how you make all your decisions.'
On lean operations after experiencing the 2008 crash: 'I will stay as lean as humanly possible for the rest of my life, which has been fun now because it's an operating system that works.'
